speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hold that up in front of you. No, the other way around. No. That's it. Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. June the 15th, 1995. Survivor-- Alexander Kronhart. Interviewer-- Fred Charatan. Place-- Boca Raton, Florida, United States of America. Language-- English. Reel one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, this is for the-- just for doc-- you know, for documentary purposes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in a sense, we're all survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judge, I'd like to you to start by telling us a little bit about your early history and your family background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was born in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- Actually, I was born December 25, 1901. But the doctor that delivered me, he took ill. And he didn't report me-- my birth until March 1. So that's the date that I officially adopted, and throughout my life used-- March 1, 1902-- as my date of birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: But actually, I was a chriskind. You know. C-H-R-I-S-K-I-N-D. Chriskind. And I owe that, I think, to the fact that I have this ESP. You know what ESP is? Extrasensory perception.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Now, when I was two years old, my mother and father and my older sister and an older brother came to New York. And we went to Castle Garden, you know, for admission. And my father settled on the East Side of New York with my older brother and my older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother was Philip, and my sister's name was Anna. My mother's name was Hannah, H-A-N-N-A-H. My father's name was Joseph. My mother's maiden name- maiden name was Hannah Smith. And she got that name by reason of the fact that her father was a blacksmith in Russia. And at that time-- and now, too-- they adopt the names according to your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trade, right. So we settled on the East Side. First, they moved into Norfolk Street, New York. 152. I remember the number. My mother was a hairdresser. My sister was a hairdresser. And my mother also made wigs, you know. Sheitels, they call them. And at 152 Norfolk Street, it was on the ground floor, tenement house. And it was an apartment on the left side. And customers used to come in to get their hair done. My sister and my mother used to do the hair. And those days, they didn't do manicuring, so it was just hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father? He was a brush manufacturer. He manufactured brush from boar-- B-O-A-R-- hair. And in fact, I have one brush here that he manufactured. And then as a kid, I was like about seven years old, and at that time, I had two twin sisters already.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were born in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born in America, yeah. That was at a previous address. And one was about a foot higher than the other. And I remember that as you enter this apartment at Norfolk Street, there was a big couch like this. And there was a shelf, on which my mother had a scale. A scale, you know? She used a scale to weigh the hair when she made wigs. And also there were little weights, like eight-ounce weights, and a pound weight, and so on. And I was asleep one day on the couch, and this younger twin sister-- she was like three years old at the time-- she climbed up on the couch, and she threw down a weight-- an eight-ounce weight-- that hit me right in this eye. And there was blood that was flowing. I felt it. And I wanted to hit her, but she got off the bed-- the couch. And my mother took me to a doctor, and he stopped the blood. And-- but later on, I found that I lost the sight in this eye. And they took me to Montefiore Hospital. And the doctor said the machinery behind the eyeball is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went through life, from a kid, and here I'm 93 years old. So I went through public school, high school, two colleges, two degrees. I drove a car since I was 18. And I have my license. You know when that expires? In 19-- no, 2000. The year 2000. But I sold my car two weeks before I came here, because I realized I wouldn't need a car. They have transportation. They have a bus, and they have cars, and so on. So getting back to this eye that I have no sight in, I think that the strength that I had in this eye went to my right eye. And in that way, I was able to go through life without any problem. I can read without my glasses. I drove my car without my glasses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And getting back to-- from Norfolk Street, we moved to 306 East Fifth Street. That was in Manhattan, near Second Avenue. And in the middle of the block down the street, there was PS 25-- Public School 25. And I attended that school. And lo and behold, when I got to the middle grades, a classmate of mine was George Gershwin, the pianist. And he was also a-- a-- a songwriter, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you friendly with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was friendly with him. And every Friday in the assembly, they had all the children from the school. There was always something going on. George Gershwin would play the piano. And after he played the piano, I recited. The teachers recognized that I had a good inflection in my voice, and they always gave me poems to recite. Like for Christmas, they give me a poem, like it would start, ''Twas the night before Christmas, and all through the house, nothing was stirring, even a mouse.' And then it went on and on. And then later, by myself-- you know, my brother was a-- he became a prize fighter later, before Benny Leonard. I think I told you. And he wanted to make a fighter out of me. And in high school, I entered the boxing tournament, and I won the bantamweight championship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was quite a-- quite a busy guy with fighting. And I was interested in fighting. And I dug out in the library a poem, And that's what I used to recite. Could you record it now? Can you recite it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 'It was the kid's last fight. No more, man. I'm quitting the ring, and I'm quitting for good. I want that understood. This right hand of mine killed the best pal I had. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's driving me crazy. It's a long story, so listen, and hear what I have to say. The kid and I [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] used to spar, and I wouldn't slug for anything. And then we used to hang out at the gym, the kid and I. And then one night, there was some swell guy hanging around the gym called Slim. He took a liking to the kid, and he signed him up. That's what he did. And then they went out on the road, and where they went, none of us knowed. But the kid changed his name, and he was looking for fortune and fame. And then, they came back to the gym with-- [INAUDIBLE] he came back to Slim-- to gym with Slim. And Slim signed us up for a bout. The crowd went wild. When we entered the ring, they stamped their feet and everything. Bang went the bell. We entered the ring, and we sparred for a while. And we kept sparring and slugging. And then all of a sudden, I stepped in close, and caught him one right on the nose. And down went the kid, for the count of three. The referee counted three. He got up at three, and then we started slugging again. And then we stepped in close again. And I caught him one, and hit him right under the chin. And down he went, and the kid was all in. The referee counted him out. That was the end of the bout. But he didn't move. And the doctor climbed into the ring, and the kid wouldn't move. He was through. And I was scared. The doctor pronounced him dead. How it happened, I don't know. But it was a blow. And it was the kid's last fight, and I'll make it mine. I'll never fight again.' And that's [? what ?] I recited at one of the entertainment. Now next Sunday, we have another performance. I sing with some ladies. And also, I sing in Spanish. And my niece here is a Spanish teacher, so I invited her to come to attend the performance. And also, at the same time, I knew that you were coming, so I invited her to come here and be present when I was being interviewed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back to your family life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of family life was it, from the cultural Jewish religious point of view?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, unfortunately, I was not religious. My father was religious. I think he had a key to every synagogue in New York. And came Saturday, he used to chase me around the block to go to the synagogue. But somehow I was irreligious. But it didn't affect my-- I knew that I had one path to go. And I knew I wanted to be a lawyer. And even though my brother was a boxer, he wanted to make a boxer out of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother, Phil?

speaker: interviewee
text: Phil, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he box under his own name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He boxed under the name of Phil 'Curly' Smith. My mother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever happen to hear of a boxer called Barney Sunshine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sunshine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sounds familiar, but I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what year did he box?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he was a patient that I had treated in the past. And he was-- he was a lightweight boxer in New York. Barney Sunshine was his boxing name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I remember his name, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, to get back to your family, did your family lack for anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father was a-- of course, there wasn't too much money. My father, he was a brush manufacturer. But in those days, they didn't make very much. But my older sister, she worked as a garment worker, you know. And they even had that big fire. You remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Triangle Shirtwaist fire disaster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's it. The Triangle. She was in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when that occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a kid. I was a kid then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it. Oh, yeah. Look, I remember way back. I remember, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your earliest memory? The earliest memory that you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: The earliest memory was I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. And I usually-- you know, my mother was busy doing hairdressing and so on. And when I came back, you know, from lunch, I went to the corner delicatessen and I got a frankfurter for one penny. And it had mustard and sauerkraut. And that's my early youth. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born during the Theodore Roosevelt administration.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of Teddy Roosevelt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember he was on horseback. And I remember he was a very determined man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he was prominent in New York City politics before he became president. Do you remember him ever coming to the Lower East Side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but you know who was in my neighborhood? Who was the congressman then?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: La Guardia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fiorello?

speaker: interviewee
text: La Guardia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fiorello La Guardia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fiorello. And he used to-- Sunday-- he became mayor, you know, later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Sunday, he used to read the funnies to the kids on the TV. And-- but I remember when he was a congressman in my district, the people that counted the votes had a ring. One man had a ring on his finger. And it had a rubber stamp, an X. So if a ballot showed a vote for the other guy, they put the X on it. So it ruled out the ballot. The ballot was void. That's how he got elected. You know, these things happen with politicians. The same as this guy D'Amato, you know? He's shrewd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, you were 12 years old when the first world war broke out. Do you have any recollections of the first world war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, tell us what you remember about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother volunteered. At that time, he was an electrician. He was like, about 19 years old, but he learned to be an electrician. And he went overseas. And he was in the engineering corps. But he took charge of the lighting. You know, the searchlights, looking for planes in the sky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said-- what I remember also about my brother, he used to get into crap games, you know? Shooting crap. And he used to send me money orders. $40, $50, and so on. And-- From France?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sent them from France. He was in the American Expeditionary Force?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. And then he came back. And then he started working for a photographer. You know, they used to put kids on the pony as they came out of school. And he used to help-- and he used to photograph them, and that's how he learned photography. And then he went out for himself. And he bought a little car, a replica of a Packard car. And he used to wait at the schools. And as the kids came out, he put them in the little car. And he went around with the proofs. And he sold them, like a dozen for $10, and so on. And then one summer when school was over, he asked me to join him. And he was going to go to Boston. And so I joined him. And believe it or not, we got to Belmont, Massachusetts. And the from the school recess, the Kennedy kids-- John F. Kennedy, Robert Kennedy, his sister, his older brother-- the one that died in the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: John.

speaker: interviewee
text: John. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joe. Joe. Yeah, he was named after the father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We photographed all these kids. Put some in the car. And I remember John F. He was like 12 years old then. He was standing in the back of their little car. And his sisters and his other brothers were alongside. And I went with the proofs to their house. And I spoke to the mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rose.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Who just died at a hundred and like, nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rose Kennedy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose. Rose, yeah. And the father, Joseph, he was there. And I sold them $20 worth of pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't suppose you've preserved any of the prints.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know if I had one of those, it would be worth a lot of money. I told my niece this morning. She asked me about it. If I had one of those, it would be worth a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, going back to your own career.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, wait a minute. Yeah. I'm coming to that. And my brother, he was a bachelor all his life, and he had wanderlust. He liked to travel. And he told me that the end of that summer, that he was going to travel to Europe. He was going to take the Italian ship. And he was going to travel to Italy, and then he was going to go all through Europe, and throughout the East. All the way to China, and Japan, and to Australia. And he wanted me to go along. He says, it'll be interesting. I said, no. I'm going back to school. I-- [? my ?] was determined to go to law school, so I went back to New York. And I finally got to NYU law school. And my brother, he traveled. I received letters from him, and cards. And when he got to Australia, my mother said, write to Phil to find a relative. My mother, when she was a little girl, she had an uncle who was in the Crimean war. And he was taken prisoner. And he was taken by the British to Australia. And my mother knew that he opened a store, and that he used to send money to them in Russia. And then he knew-- my mother knew that he got married. And then all of a sudden, she stopped hearing from him. She knew something happened. So when my brother went to Australia, my mother said, write a letter to Phil to find her relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my brother, Phil, wrote back saying, you have enough relatives in Brooklyn. And even if you find them, you're not going to see them. So what's the point?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I got a letter later. He says, Al, I found the relatives. Not that I went looking for them. But on the main street-- Commonwealth Avenue in Melbourne-- I saw a sign on a store that said, 'Smith's.' Smith's. It's a general store, like a department store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in, and I saw the owner. Tall, dignified-looking man. I got pictures of him here. And he said, I don't know about any relatives, in Brooklyn or New York or anywheres in the US. But my older sister, she would know. So he got on the phone, and he called for his sister. And she came down with a livery chauffeur. And my brother took one look at her, and he almost went through the ground. The spitting image of my mother! And they wined him and dined him. And then they wanted to make a shidduch. Do you know what a shidduch is? I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For him, and so he disappeared. He went back to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wanted to retain his status as a confirmed bachelor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, when did you graduate from NYU law school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated in 19-- I had one college degree before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took business courses. I got a degree called BCS, Bachelor of Commercial Science. It's the same as an MA, Master of Administration. So I got that degree first. And then in 1926, I graduated law school. And that year, I took the bar examination. I passed. And I got my admission to the bar in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went into the private practice of law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went into private practice of law. I happen to have a friend who was a bail bondsman. And he recommended cases to me. That was during prohibition already. And by the way, that shows. And there, the assistant district attorney in charge of the cases was Mickey Marcus. Does that ring a bell?

speaker: interviewer
text: It does.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dave Marcus. And in the war, he was Colonel Dave Marcus. He went to West Point. And he was a wrestler. Good wrestler. And I was a boxer. So we became good friends. And when I went to Washington, I met Mickey Marcus. And he's the one that introduced me to General Clay. You see this whole thing was like a dream to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: A panorama.

speaker: interviewee
text: Panorama. And then of course, another thing that I consider as fate. There was a hotel in Strasburg, Pennsylvania. And one summer, a weekend in July-- that would be like 1932-- I went with three or four friends of mine for a weekend, a July 4th weekend. And I went to the-- this hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after the weekend, my friends and I came back. It was the Lackawanna Railroad that went from Strasburg to New York. And on this train, I was sitting with my friends, and all of a sudden, I see-- hold it? Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold it? Yeah. I see a woman walking up the aisle. She had a white headband, a white dress, and a gold belt. And she was handing out sandwiches. She had gotten her lunch, and she offered. She said, anybody want a sandwich? And it so happened that my friends and I, we had lunch. But they gave her this lunch [INAUDIBLE], and she was handing it out. She looked like an angel to me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then nothing happened. We got to New York. I went back home. My friends went home. And this lady I thought I'd never see again. But it so happened-- and this, again, is fate-- a month or two later, I decided to move my family. See, I was supporting my family by that time. My father was retired. My mother was retired. My sister, well, she was sort of semi-retired. My brother, he was traveling all away to California, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a cousin who was a doctor. Dr. Smith, my mother's nephew. And he had an office on Eastern Parkway and Franklin Avenue. So I decided to move my family in that area.
